completeness.rationale: The triage captured most expected information: memory growth from ~500MB to 4GB+, the v2.3 upgrade trigger, ~200 active users, consistent daily pattern, restart as workaround, and the notification system changelog entry. Missing: explicit mention of server specs (8GB RAM VM, Ubuntu 22.04). The triage went beyond expected by capturing listener count data from /debug/vars.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis matches the actual cause precisely: event listeners registered per API call in the v2.3 notification system, never removed. The agent correctly identified the leak mechanism, the v2.3 regression, and even the specific evidence (listener count growing from ~200 to 40,000+). This exceeds even the acceptable diagnostic paths by pinpointing the exact mechanism.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked about process-level vs OS-level memory (confirming the right process), regression timing (surfacing the v2.3 upgrade), activity correlation (confirming per-request leak), changelog review (finding the notification system change), and finally WebSocket/listener counts (directly confirming the mechanism). No obvious follow-up questions were skipped given the information available.

economy.rationale: Four turns of questioning, each building directly on the prior answer. No redundant questions. The WebSocket connection check in turn 3 was a reasonable diagnostic step even though it turned out connections weren't the issue — it led the reporter to discover the /debug/vars listener count data, which was the smoking gun.

question_quality.rationale: Questions demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning. The first question distinguished process vs. host memory and checked for regressions. The second checked activity correlation (distinguishing request-rate leak from time-based leak) and specifically asked about the changelog with targeted subsystems. The third question about WebSocket connections was hypothesis-driven and led the reporter to discover the /debug/vars evidence unprompted. Each question narrowed the hypothesis space effectively.

actionability.rationale: A developer could start coding a fix immediately. The summary specifies: diff v2.3 vs v2.2 notification subsystem, look for emitter.on() without removeListener(), the fix is to register once per session or remove after use. A proposed test case with specific assertions is included. The reproduction steps are concrete and verifiable.

notable_strengths: Immediately connected the v2.3 'improved real-time notifications' changelog entry to the memory leak hypothesis — showing genuine domain knowledge about common leak patterns
The WebSocket connection count question, while not confirming the hypothesis directly, prompted the reporter to investigate /debug/vars and discover the listener count evidence themselves
The final summary includes a concrete proposed test case with specific assertions, making it immediately useful for preventing regressions
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask about server specs (RAM, OS), which is part of the expected information and relevant for understanding severity and headroom
Could have asked whether a config option existed to disable the new notification system as an immediate mitigation, rather than listing it as an information gap
most_insightful_question: Does the memory growth correlate with user activity, or does it happen even when the system is idle? — This distinguished a per-request leak from a time-based or background-process leak, and combined with the changelog question in the same turn, efficiently triangulated the notification system as the culprit.
missed_opportunities: Server hardware/OS specs (RAM total, OS version) — relevant for severity assessment and not captured
Whether the reporter had tried disabling or configuring the new notification feature as a workaround short of daily restarts
